_metadata.purpose: Golden fixture for verify_interactions.py + build_interaction_db.py byte-identity tests. Every row has verified RXCUIs (from scripts/data/drug_classes.json) and CUIs (from scripts/data/ingredient_quality_map.json) plus an authoritative NIH/NCCIH/LiverTox/DailyMed URL. Never shipped to production — test fixture only.
sources: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements (ods.od.nih.gov) — supplement fact sheets
NIH NCCIH (nccih.nih.gov) — herbal and integrative health
_metadata.notes: Severities use draft vocabulary (Contraindicated/Major/Moderate/Minor). verify_interactions.py normalizes to Flutter enum (contraindicated/avoid/caution/monitor). All Major+ entries have ≥1 source_url to pass the evidence gate (§6.2 check 9).

interactions.mechanism: Vitamin K is the cofactor for vitamin K-dependent clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X). Exogenous vitamin K directly opposes warfarin's anticoagulant effect and lowers INR.
interactions.management: Maintain consistent daily vitamin K intake. Avoid sudden increases from supplements or large servings of leafy greens. Monitor INR more frequently when starting or stopping any vitamin K source.

interactions.mechanism: Ginkgo biloba inhibits platelet-activating factor and has additive antiplatelet effects when combined with warfarin, increasing bleeding risk independent of INR.
interactions.management: Avoid combining ginkgo with warfarin. If already taking both, discuss with your clinician and monitor for bruising, nosebleeds, or unusual bleeding.

interactions.mechanism: Garlic supplements (especially aged or high-allicin extracts) have antiplatelet activity that can compound warfarin's bleeding risk.
interactions.management: Culinary amounts of garlic are safe. Avoid concentrated garlic supplements while on warfarin. Watch for easy bruising or bleeding.

interactions.mechanism: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) have mild antiplatelet activity. At doses above 3 g/day, bleeding risk increases when combined with warfarin.
interactions.management: Doses under 3 g/day are generally safe on warfarin. Higher doses require clinician oversight and more frequent INR checks.

interactions.mechanism: St. John's Wort is a potent inducer of CYP3A4, CYP2C9, and P-glycoprotein. It accelerates warfarin metabolism, lowering plasma levels and INR — loss of anticoagulation.
interactions.management: Do not combine St. John's Wort with warfarin. If already taking both, speak to your clinician before stopping either — INR will shift.

interactions.mechanism: Coenzyme Q10 is structurally similar to vitamin K and may reduce warfarin's anticoagulant effect, lowering INR in some patients.
interactions.management: Avoid starting or stopping CoQ10 without monitoring INR. Maintain a consistent dose once stable.

interactions.mechanism: NSAIDs inhibit platelet COX-1. High-dose omega-3 fatty acids add antiplatelet effect, compounding bleeding risk — especially GI bleeding.
interactions.management: Limit fish oil to under 3 g/day if using NSAIDs regularly. Avoid chronic combined use without clinician guidance.

interactions.mechanism: Ginkgo's antiplatelet effect compounds with NSAID-induced COX inhibition, increasing risk of GI bleeding and ecchymosis.
interactions.management: Avoid chronic co-use. Stop ginkgo at least 36 hours before any NSAID-heavy surgical or dental procedure.

interactions.mechanism: ACE inhibitors reduce aldosterone, causing potassium retention. Added potassium from supplements or salt substitutes can push serum potassium into dangerous hyperkalemia.
interactions.management: Do not take potassium supplements or potassium-based salt substitutes with ACE inhibitors unless directed by a clinician. Monitor serum potassium.

interactions.mechanism: Potassium-sparing diuretics (spironolactone, amiloride, triamterene) reduce potassium excretion. Supplemental potassium can cause hyperkalemia. Loop/thiazide diuretics behave differently but combined regimens are common.
interactions.management: Avoid potassium supplements unless a clinician has checked serum potassium and is monitoring levels.

interactions.mechanism: St. John's Wort induces CYP3A4, accelerating metabolism of CYP3A4-substrate statins (atorvastatin, simvastatin, lovastatin) and reducing their lipid-lowering effect.
interactions.management: Avoid St. John's Wort with CYP3A4-metabolized statins. Pravastatin and rosuvastatin are less affected but still require clinician review.

interactions.mechanism: St. John's Wort inhibits serotonin reuptake and has MAO-A activity. Combined with SSRIs, the additive serotonergic load can precipitate serotonin syndrome.
interactions.management: Do not combine St. John's Wort with SSRIs. Watch for agitation, hyperreflexia, tremor, fever, or confusion if inadvertently co-used.

interactions.mechanism: 5-HTP is the direct precursor to serotonin. Added to an SSRI, serotonin synthesis and synaptic availability both increase, creating serotonin syndrome risk.
interactions.management: Avoid combining 5-HTP with SSRIs. Any change must be clinician-supervised with adequate washout between agents.

interactions.mechanism: MAOIs block monoamine oxidase. St. John's Wort has weak MAO inhibition plus serotonin reuptake inhibition. Combined use risks serotonin syndrome and hypertensive crisis.
interactions.management: Do not combine. A 14-day washout is required when switching from one agent to the other.

interactions.mechanism: MAOIs prevent degradation of serotonin. 5-HTP drives serotonin synthesis. Combined, serotonin accumulates rapidly — life-threatening serotonin syndrome.
interactions.management: Never combine 5-HTP with MAOIs. A 14-day washout is required after stopping an MAOI before starting 5-HTP.

interactions.mechanism: Calcium competes with non-heme iron for absorption at the enterocyte DMT1 transporter. Co-ingestion of calcium ≥300 mg can reduce iron absorption by up to 60 percent.
interactions.management: Separate iron and calcium supplements by at least 2 hours. Take iron on an empty stomach with vitamin C when possible.

interactions.mechanism: Iron and zinc share intestinal transport pathways. When taken together at supplemental doses (iron:zinc ratio >2:1), zinc absorption drops significantly.
interactions.management: Separate iron and zinc supplements by at least 2 hours. Food can reduce the competition but also reduces iron absorption.

interactions.mechanism: High-dose calcium (≥600 mg) reduces zinc absorption modestly, likely via shared divalent cation transport.
interactions.management: Separate calcium and zinc doses by 2 hours if both are supplemented at high levels. Dietary amounts are not a concern.

interactions.mechanism: NSAIDs inhibit platelet aggregation and cause GI mucosal erosion. Combined with warfarin's anticoagulation, GI bleeding risk rises dramatically. Some NSAIDs also displace warfarin from albumin, raising free drug levels.
interactions.management: Avoid chronic NSAID use on warfarin. If short-term use is necessary, use the lowest effective dose with a PPI and INR monitoring.

interactions.mechanism: NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandin synthesis, decreasing lithium clearance and raising serum lithium toward toxic levels (>1.5 mEq/L).
interactions.management: Avoid chronic NSAID use with lithium. If needed, check lithium level within 5–7 days of starting and adjust dose.
